title: Ghosts Betwixt - Unboxing
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_fcTS5NosDs

Daniel (@dungeondive) unboxes the retail Kickstarter edition of The Ghosts Betwixt: Chapter One, having previewed the prototype years earlier — and, full disclosure, designer Dustin Frond is a friend who likely upgraded his copy to deluxe. The 90s-horror family dungeon crawl arrives well-packed: custom dice in a bag, double-sided standees (with alternate monster art — mom bats, jackoons, guard dogs), stickers for numbering enemy bases, and trimmed-down character sheets for Maddox, Avalyn, Bill and Joan. Daniel's enthusiasm keeps circling back to the loot, a huge deck of household weapons and junk — slingshots, sulfuric acid, TV dinners, milk and OJ for healing — that fits the rundown-farmhouse fiction perfectly. He's visibly reassured by the documentation: a split quick-play guide and rules reference, a full component showcase, and what he calls possibly the best index he's ever seen in a board game, doubling as a glossary with definitions and page numbers. That attention to detail, he says, is the same thing that gave him confidence in Sleeping Gods. He flips (spoiler-lightly) through nine missions' worth of tiles, enemy cards including oddly-named human 'monsters,' and the tiered equipment decks you unlock as you play.

----

Daniel says a great index and glossary fills him with confidence before he's even learned a game. What component or design touch signals 'this designer sweated the details' to you before the first play?
